summary: Jeff Atwood endorses Joel Spolsky's response to a programmer considering leaving the industry, arguing that programming remains an excellent career with great pay and creative fulfillment. He observes that economic downturns serve a useful purpose by weeding out those who entered the field for the wrong reasons, leaving behind the passionate developers who truly love their work. Atwood contends that programmers who question whether they should stay in the field are probably answering their own question. He acknowledges this might sound harsh but argues it benefits everyone when uncommitted developers make room for those with genuine passion. He closes by admitting his own bias, noting he spent his Christmas vacation writing code for Stack Overflow.
key_points: Programming offers exceptional compensation and creative fulfillment compared to most careers, with six-figure salaries common for those with just bachelor's degrees
Economic downturns beneficially filter out 'careerist gold diggers' who entered programming for money rather than passion
Too many programmers 'had no business being paid to be a programmer' and actively harm the profession and their coworkers
If a programmer ever hints they might want to leave the field, they should be encouraged to do so
The best programmers have a lifelong passion that no economic conditions could shake
money_quotes: Programming: love it or leave it.
I mean this in the nicest possible way, but not everyone should be a programmer.
I've worked with far too many programmers who honestly had no business being paid to be a programmer.
One of the (very) few bright spots of the previous bubble was that it weeded out all the people who didn't truly love software development.
The only people left applying for programming jobs were the true freaks and geeks who, y'know, loved this stuff.
If a programmer ever hints, even in passing, that they might possibly want to exit the field – they probably should.
The best programmers I've known have all had a lifelong passion for what they do. There's no way a minor economic blip would ever convince them they should do anything else.
key_insight: Programming should be reserved for those with genuine passion for the craft, and economic downturns usefully reveal who those people really are.
